title: Offer evaluation - Evaluation process | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Apply the phased offer compliance process
Determine if a peer review is required

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Offer evaluation - Evaluation process
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Offer evaluation - Evaluation process
structure_blocks.text: Evaluation process The evaluation of offers must be completed according to the procedures outlined in the solicitation. Offers must be reviewed to ensure they meet the contractual, technical and financial requirements of the solicitation. Fair, accurate, and transparent evaluation of offers is an important aspect of the procurement process. Financial offers must not be shared with the technical evaluators until after completion of the technical evaluation. The client is responsible for the evaluation of the technical portion of the offer and the management portion where applicable. You are responsible for the evaluation of contractual terms and conditions and the financial portion of the offers. Offers must meet all the mandatory requirements set out in the solicitation. Failure to meet any mandatory requirement or mandatory evaluation criteria, whether technical, financial or other, will result in the offer being declared non-responsive. For complexity levels 2 and 3, the procurement risk assessment may determine that a formal peer review of the results of the offer evaluation is required. For complexity levels 4 and 5, a formal review of the results of the evaluation is mandatory. In all other situations, once the offer evaluation is completed you are encouraged to obtain a review of the results of the evaluation prior to issuing the contract. The Evaluator’s Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Certification (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) document must be completed as part of all PSPC-led solicitation processes that involve an evaluation of offers. When third parties such as scholars or experts will be involved in offer evaluation, offerors must be informed in the solicitation. Third parties must sign non-disclosure and conflict of interest agreements before participating in evaluation or offer preparation. Consistency in evaluators should be maintained when possible. A thorough review of offers is essential to avoid missing crucial information. Evaluations should not be influenced by criteria or factors not specified in the solicitation or derive conclusions from information contained in offers that may prove wrong. Documents pertaining to the evaluation of offers must be retained. Evaluators must provide the original or a copy of all evaluation notes and communications to you to use when filling in the procurement file. All such information is subject to the Access to Information Act . Evaluators’ worksheets must not be destroyed even if the information contained in the worksheets is recorded in other evaluation documents. Destruction of the evaluators' worksheets is a breach of international trade agreements. While the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) does not contain a similar provision, maintaining complete documentation is crucial under the CFTA to ensure fair and open procurement procedures. You may consult the Treasury Board Directive on Recordkeeping for further clarification.

raw_text:
Evaluation process The evaluation of offers must be completed according to the procedures outlined in the solicitation. Offers must be reviewed to ensure they meet the contractual, technical and financial requirements of the solicitation. Fair, accurate, and transparent evaluation of offers is an important aspect of the procurement process. Financial offers must not be shared with the technical evaluators until after completion of the technical evaluation. The client is responsible for the evaluation of the technical portion of the offer and the management portion where applicable. You are responsible for the evaluation of contractual terms and conditions and the financial portion of the offers. Offers must meet all the mandatory requirements set out in the solicitation. Failure to meet any mandatory requirement or mandatory evaluation criteria, whether technical, financial or other, will result in the offer being declared non-responsive. For complexity levels 2 and 3, the procurement risk assessment may determine that a formal peer review of the results of the offer evaluation is required. For complexity levels 4 and 5, a formal review of the results of the evaluation is mandatory. In all other situations, once the offer evaluation is completed you are encouraged to obtain a review of the results of the evaluation prior to issuing the contract. The Evaluator’s Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Certification (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) document must be completed as part of all PSPC-led solicitation processes that involve an evaluation of offers. When third parties such as scholars or experts will be involved in offer evaluation, offerors must be informed in the solicitation. Third parties must sign non-disclosure and conflict of interest agreements before participating in evaluation or offer preparation. Consistency in evaluators should be maintained when possible. A thorough review of offers is essential to avoid missing crucial information. Evaluations should not be influenced by criteria or factors not specified in the solicitation or derive conclusions from information contained in offers that may prove wrong. Documents pertaining to the evaluation of offers must be retained. Evaluators must provide the original or a copy of all evaluation notes and communications to you to use when filling in the procurement file. All such information is subject to the Access to Information Act . Evaluators’ worksheets must not be destroyed even if the information contained in the worksheets is recorded in other evaluation documents. Destruction of the evaluators' worksheets is a breach of international trade agreements. While the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) does not contain a similar provision, maintaining complete documentation is crucial under the CFTA to ensure fair and open procurement procedures. You may consult the Treasury Board Directive on Recordkeeping for further clarification.

Evaluation process

The evaluation of offers must be completed according to the procedures outlined in the solicitation. Offers must be reviewed to ensure they meet the contractual, technical and financial requirements of the solicitation. Fair, accurate, and transparent evaluation of offers is an important aspect of the procurement process.

Financial offers must not be shared with the technical evaluators until after completion of the technical evaluation. The client is responsible for the evaluation of the technical portion of the offer and the management portion where applicable.

You are responsible for the evaluation of contractual terms and conditions and the financial portion of the offers. Offers must meet all the mandatory requirements set out in the solicitation. Failure to meet any mandatory requirement or mandatory evaluation criteria, whether technical, financial or other, will result in the offer being declared non-responsive.

For complexity levels 2 and 3, the procurement risk assessment may determine that a formal peer review of the results of the offer evaluation is required. For complexity levels 4 and 5, a formal review of the results of the evaluation is mandatory. In all other situations, once the offer evaluation is completed you are encouraged to obtain a review of the results of the evaluation prior to issuing the contract.

The Evaluator’s Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Certification (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) document must be completed as part of all PSPC-led solicitation processes that involve an evaluation of offers.

When third parties such as scholars or experts will be involved in offer evaluation, offerors must be informed in the solicitation. Third parties must sign non-disclosure and conflict of interest agreements before participating in evaluation or offer preparation.

Consistency in evaluators should be maintained when possible. A thorough review of offers is essential to avoid missing crucial information. Evaluations should not be influenced by criteria or factors not specified in the solicitation or derive conclusions from information contained in offers that may prove wrong.

Documents pertaining to the evaluation of offers must be retained. Evaluators must provide the original or a copy of all evaluation notes and communications to you to use when filling in the procurement file. All such information is subject to the Access to Information Act .

Evaluators’ worksheets must not be destroyed even if the information contained in the worksheets is recorded in other evaluation documents. Destruction of the evaluators' worksheets is a breach of international trade agreements.

While the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) does not contain a similar provision, maintaining complete documentation is crucial under the CFTA to ensure fair and open procurement procedures. You may consult the Treasury Board Directive on Recordkeeping for further clarification.
